question:
3. Evaluate
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{j=1}^{n^{2}} \frac{n}{n^{2}+j^{2}} .
\]
solution:
Solution. We write the sum in the form
\[
S_{n}=\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n^{2}} \frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{i}{n}\right)^{2}} .
\]

Since
\[
\int_{i / n}^{(i+1) / n} \frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}<\frac{1}{n} \frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{i}{n}\right)^{2}}<\int_{(i-1) / n}^{1 / n} \frac{d x}{1+x^{2}} .
\]
we get
\[
\int_{1 / n}^{\left(n^{2}+1\right) / n} \frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}<S_{n}<\int_{0}^{n} \frac{d x}{1+x^{2}} .
\]

Now
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\int_{0}^{n} \frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}=\arctan n . \quad \lim _{n-\infty} \arctan n=\frac{\pi}{2} \\
\int_{1 \cdot n}^{n+\left(11^{\prime} n\right)} \frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}=\arctan \left(n+\frac{1}{n}\right)-\arctan \left(\frac{1}{n}\right) .
\end{array}
\]
and
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\arctan \left(n+\frac{1}{n}\right)-\arctan \left(\frac{1}{n}\right)\right)=\frac{\pi}{2} .
\]

Thus both the left and right members of (1) have the limit \( \pi / 2 \), so
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} S_{n}=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]

descriptive_long.question:
3. Evaluate
\[
\lim _{biginteger \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{indexvar=1}^{biginteger^{2}} \frac{biginteger}{biginteger^{2}+indexvar^{2}} .
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We write the sum in the form
\[
sumvalue=\frac{1}{biginteger} \sum_{iterator=1}^{biginteger^{2}} \frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{iterator}{biginteger}\right)^{2}} .
\]

Since
\[
\int_{iterator / biginteger}^{(iterator+1) / biginteger} \frac{d realaxis}{1+realaxis^{2}}<\frac{1}{biginteger} \frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{iterator}{biginteger}\right)^{2}}<\int_{(iterator-1) / biginteger}^{1 / biginteger} \frac{d realaxis}{1+realaxis^{2}} .
\]
we get
\[
\int_{1 / biginteger}^{\left(biginteger^{2}+1\right) / biginteger} \frac{d realaxis}{1+realaxis^{2}}<sumvalue<\int_{0}^{biginteger} \frac{d realaxis}{1+realaxis^{2}} .
\]

Now
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\int_{0}^{biginteger} \frac{d realaxis}{1+realaxis^{2}}=\arctan biginteger . \quad \lim _{biginteger-\infty} \arctan biginteger=\frac{\pi}{2} \\
\int_{1 \cdot biginteger}^{biginteger+\left(11^{\prime} biginteger\right)} \frac{d realaxis}{1+realaxis^{2}}=\arctan \left(biginteger+\frac{1}{biginteger}\right)-\arctan \left(\frac{1}{biginteger}\right) .
\end{array}
\]
and
\[
\lim _{biginteger \rightarrow \infty}\left(\arctan \left(biginteger+\frac{1}{biginteger}\right)-\arctan \left(\frac{1}{biginteger}\right)\right)=\frac{\pi}{2} .
\]

Thus both the left and right members of (1) have the limit \( \pi / 2 \), so
\[
\lim _{biginteger \rightarrow \infty} sumvalue=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
\[
\lim _{elephant \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{harmonica=1}^{elephant^{2}} \frac{elephant}{elephant^{2}+harmonica^{2}} .
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We write the sum in the form
\[
watermelon=\frac{1}{elephant} \sum_{i=1}^{elephant^{2}} \frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{i}{elephant}\right)^{2}} .
\]

Since
\[
\int_{i / elephant}^{(i+1) / elephant} \frac{d \zeppelin}{1+\zeppelin^{2}}<\frac{1}{elephant} \frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{i}{elephant}\right)^{2}}<\int_{(i-1) / elephant}^{1 / elephant} \frac{d \zeppelin}{1+\zeppelin^{2}} .
\]
we get
\[
\int_{1 / elephant}^{\left(elephant^{2}+1\right) / elephant} \frac{d \zeppelin}{1+\zeppelin^{2}}<watermelon<\int_{0}^{elephant} \frac{d \zeppelin}{1+\zeppelin^{2}} .
\]

Now
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\int_{0}^{elephant} \frac{d \zeppelin}{1+\zeppelin^{2}}=\arctan elephant . \quad \lim _{elephant-\infty} \arctan elephant=\frac{\pi}{2} \\
\int_{1 \cdot elephant}^{elephant+\left(11^{\prime} elephant\right)} \frac{d \zeppelin}{1+\zeppelin^{2}}=\arctan \left(elephant+\frac{1}{elephant}\right)-\arctan \left(\frac{1}{elephant}\right) .
\end{array}
\]
and
\[
\lim _{elephant \rightarrow \infty}\left(\arctan \left(elephant+\frac{1}{elephant}\right)-\arctan \left(\frac{1}{elephant}\right)\right)=\frac{\pi}{2} .
\]

Thus both the left and right members of (1) have the limit \( \pi / 2 \), so
\[
\lim _{elephant \rightarrow \infty} watermelon=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
3. Evaluate
\[
\lim _{infinitesimal \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{colossal=1}^{infinitesimal^{2}} \frac{infinitesimal}{infinitesimal^{2}+colossal^{2}} .
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We write the sum in the form
\[
difference=\frac{1}{infinitesimal} \sum_{enormous=1}^{infinitesimal^{2}} \frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{enormous}{infinitesimal}\right)^{2}} .
\]

Since
\[
\int_{enormous / infinitesimal}^{(enormous+1) / infinitesimal} \frac{d dependent}{1+dependent^{2}}<\frac{1}{infinitesimal} \frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{enormous}{infinitesimal}\right)^{2}}<\int_{(enormous-1) / infinitesimal}^{1 / infinitesimal} \frac{d dependent}{1+dependent^{2}} .
\]
we get
\[
\int_{1 / infinitesimal}^{\left(infinitesimal^{2}+1\right) / infinitesimal} \frac{d dependent}{1+dependent^{2}}<difference<\int_{0}^{infinitesimal} \frac{d dependent}{1+dependent^{2}} .
\]

Now
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\int_{0}^{infinitesimal} \frac{d dependent}{1+dependent^{2}}=\arctan infinitesimal . \quad \lim _{infinitesimal-\infty} \arctan infinitesimal=\frac{\pi}{2} \\
\int_{1 \cdot infinitesimal}^{infinitesimal+\left(11^{\prime} infinitesimal\right)} \frac{d dependent}{1+dependent^{2}}=\arctan \left(infinitesimal+\frac{1}{infinitesimal}\right)-\arctan \left(\frac{1}{infinitesimal}\right) .
\end{array}
\]
and
\[
\lim _{infinitesimal \rightarrow \infty}\left(\arctan \left(infinitesimal+\frac{1}{infinitesimal}\right)-\arctan \left(\frac{1}{infinitesimal}\right)\right)=\frac{\pi}{2} .
\]

Thus both the left and right members of (1) have the limit \( \pi / 2 \), so
\[
\lim _{infinitesimal \rightarrow \infty} difference=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]

garbled_string.question:
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{hjgrksla=1}^{qzxwvtnp^{2}} \frac{qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}} .
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We write the sum in the form
\[
vbxkqplm=\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} \sum_{cnsdyhrg=1}^{qzxwvtnp^{2}} \frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{cnsdyhrg}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{2}} .
\]

Since
\[
\int_{cnsdyhrg / qzxwvtnp}^{(cnsdyhrg+1) / qzxwvtnp} \frac{d twpqlkjs}{1+twpqlkjs^{2}}<\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} \frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{cnsdyhrg}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{2}}<\int_{(cnsdyhrg-1) / qzxwvtnp}^{1 / qzxwvtnp} \frac{d twpqlkjs}{1+twpqlkjs^{2}} .
\]
we get
\[
\int_{1 / qzxwvtnp}^{\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+1\right) / qzxwvtnp} \frac{d twpqlkjs}{1+twpqlkjs^{2}}<vbxkqplm<\int_{0}^{qzxwvtnp} \frac{d twpqlkjs}{1+twpqlkjs^{2}} .
\]

Now
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\int_{0}^{qzxwvtnp} \frac{d twpqlkjs}{1+twpqlkjs^{2}}=\arctan qzxwvtnp . \quad \lim _{qzxwvtnp-\infty} \arctan qzxwvtnp=\frac{\pi}{2} \\
\int_{1 \cdot qzxwvtnp}^{qzxwvtnp+\left(11^{\prime} qzxwvtnp\right)} \frac{d twpqlkjs}{1+twpqlkjs^{2}}=\arctan \left(qzxwvtnp+\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)-\arctan \left(\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\right) .
\end{array}
\]
and
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty}\left(\arctan \left(qzxwvtnp+\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)-\arctan \left(\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)\right)=\frac{\pi}{2} .
\]

Thus both the left and right members of (1) have the limit \( \pi / 2 \), so
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty} vbxkqplm=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
f(x)=\frac{1}{1+x^{2}},\qquad x\ge 0 .
\]

For every positive integer \(n\) put  
\[
S_{n}\;=\;\frac{1}{n^{2}}
\sum_{\substack{1\le i,j\le n^{2}\\\gcd(i,j)=1}}
f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{i}{n}\Bigr)\,
f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{j}{n}\Bigr).
\]

Evaluate the limit  
\[
L \;=\;\lim_{n\to\infty} S_{n}.
\]

(The Mobius function is denoted by \(\mu\), the Riemann zeta-function by \(\zeta\).)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout \(C,C_{1},\dots\) denote positive absolute constants that may vary from line to line, and  
\[
\int f:=\int_{0}^{\infty}\!f(x)\,dx=\frac{\pi}{2}.
\]

Step 1. Mobius inversion.  
Using the identity \(\mathbf{1}_{(\gcd(i,j)=1)}=\sum_{d\mid i,\,d\mid j}\mu(d)\), we obtain  
\[
S_{n}
=\frac{1}{n^{2}}
\sum_{d=1}^{n^{2}}\mu(d)
\sum_{k=1}^{\lfloor n^{2}/d\rfloor}
\sum_{\ell=1}^{\lfloor n^{2}/d\rfloor}
f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{dk}{n}\Bigr)\,
f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{d\ell}{n}\Bigr). \tag{1}
\]

Put \(m_{d}(n):=\lfloor n^{2}/d\rfloor\).  
The inner double sum factors:
\[
\sum_{k,\ell\le m_{d}(n)}f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{dk}{n}\Bigr)
f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{d\ell}{n}\Bigr)
=\Bigl[\;\sum_{k=1}^{m_{d}(n)}f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{dk}{n}\Bigr)\Bigr]^{2}. \tag{2}
\]

Step 2. A uniform Riemann-sum estimate.  
Define  
\[
A_{d,n}:=\frac{d}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{m_{d}(n)}f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{dk}{n}\Bigr).
\]

Because \(f\) is positive, decreasing and continuous, for every \(k\ge 1\) we have  
\[
\int_{(k-1)d/n}^{kd/n}\!f(x)\,dx
\;\ge\;\frac{d}{n}f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{kd}{n}\Bigr)
\;\ge\;
\int_{kd/n}^{(k+1)d/n}\!f(x)\,dx .
\]

Summing \(k=1,\dots,m_{d}(n)\) yields  
\[
\int_{d/n}^{(m_{d}(n)+1)d/n}\!f
\;\le\;A_{d,n}
\;\le\;
\int_{0}^{m_{d}(n)d/n}\!f. \tag{3}
\]

Hence  
\[
\Bigl|A_{d,n}-\!\!\int_{0}^{m_{d}(n)d/n}\!f\Bigr|
\le 2\frac{d}{n}. \tag{4}
\]

We now show that
\[
m_{d}(n)\,\frac{d}{n}\;\ge\;\frac{n}{2}\qquad(n\ge 2,\;1\le d\le n^{2}). \tag{5}
\]

Indeed, since \(m_{d}(n)=\lfloor n^{2}/d\rfloor\ge n^{2}/d-1\),  
\[
m_{d}(n)\frac{d}{n}\;\ge\;\Bigl(\frac{n^{2}}{d}-1\Bigr)\frac{d}{n}
=\;n-\frac{d}{n}.
\]
If \(d\le n^{2}/2\) the right-hand side is at least \(n/2\).  
If \(d> n^{2}/2\) then \(m_{d}(n)=1\) and \(m_{d}(n)\tfrac{d}{n}=d/n\ge n^{2}/(2n)=n/2\).  
Thus (5) holds in all cases.

Because \(f(x)\le x^{-2}\) for \(x\ge 1\) and \(n/2\ge 1\) when \(n\ge 2\), (5) gives  
\[
\int_{m_{d}(n)d/n}^{\infty}f(x)\,dx
\;\le\;\int_{n/2}^{\infty}x^{-2}\,dx
=\frac{2}{n}. \tag{6}
\]

Combining (4) and (6) we obtain  
\[
\bigl|A_{d,n}-\tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr|
\le 2\frac{d}{n}+\frac{2}{n}\le 4\frac{d}{n}. \tag{7}
\]

A second fact needed later is the trivial bound  
\[
0\le A_{d,n}\le\int_{0}^{\infty}f=\frac{\pi}{2}. \tag{8}
\]

Step 3. Separating the main term.  
Rewrite the inner sum as  
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{m_{d}(n)}f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{dk}{n}\Bigr)
=\frac{n}{d}\Bigl(\frac{\pi}{2}+\varepsilon_{d,n}\Bigr),\qquad
|\varepsilon_{d,n}|\le 4\frac{d}{n}, \tag{9}
\]
by (7).  
Insert (9) into (1)-(2):
\[
S_{n}
=\sum_{d=1}^{n^{2}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}
\Bigl(\frac{\pi}{2}+\varepsilon_{d,n}\Bigr)^{2}. \tag{10}
\]

Step 4. Passage to the limit.

(i) Main term.  
\[
\sum_{d\le n^{2}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}
\;\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}\;
\sum_{d=1}^{\infty}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}
=\frac{1}{\zeta(2)}
=\frac{6}{\pi^{2}}. \tag{11}
\]

(ii) Linear error term.  
Using \(|\varepsilon_{d,n}|\le 4d/n\),
\[
\Bigl|\sum_{d\le n^{2}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}\varepsilon_{d,n}\Bigr|
\le\frac{4}{n}\sum_{d\le n^{2}}\frac{1}{d}
=O\!\Bigl(\tfrac{\log n}{n}\Bigr)\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}0. \tag{12}
\]

(iii) Quadratic error term.  
Define  
\[
Q_{n}:=\sum_{d\le n^{2}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}\varepsilon_{d,n}^{2}. \tag{13}
\]

Absolute convergence of \(\sum_{d\ge 1}|\,\mu(d)|/d^{2}\) is crucial.  
From (8) we have \(|\varepsilon_{d,n}|\le M:=\pi/2\).  
Fix \(\eta>0\); choose \(D=D(\eta)\) such that  
\[
\sum_{d>D}\frac{|\,\mu(d)|}{d^{2}}<\frac{\eta}{M^{2}}. \tag{14}
\]

Split (13) at \(D\):
\[
Q_{n}=Q_{n}^{(1)}+Q_{n}^{(2)},\quad
Q_{n}^{(1)}:=\sum_{d\le D}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}\varepsilon_{d,n}^{2},\;
Q_{n}^{(2)}:=\sum_{D<d\le n^{2}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}\varepsilon_{d,n}^{2}.
\]

For each fixed \(d\) we have \(\varepsilon_{d,n}\to 0\) by (9); hence \(Q_{n}^{(1)}\to 0\).  
For the tail use (14):
\[
|Q_{n}^{(2)}|\le M^{2}\sum_{d>D}\frac{|\,\mu(d)|}{d^{2}}<\eta. \tag{15}
\]

Because \(\eta\) is arbitrary, (15) forces \(Q_{n}^{(2)}\to 0\), thus \(Q_{n}\to 0\).

Step 5. Conclusion.  
Combining (10)-(12) and the above limit for \(Q_{n}\) we get  
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}S_{n}
=\Bigl(\frac{\pi}{2}\Bigr)^{2}\cdot\frac{1}{\zeta(2)}
=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}\cdot\frac{6}{\pi^{2}}
=\frac{3}{2}.
\]
\[
\boxed{L=\dfrac{3}{2}}
\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\;\;\;\;\square
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension: the one–dimensional Riemann sum is replaced by a two–dimensional sum, drastically enlarging the combinatorial range.  
• Additional constraint: the requirement \(\gcd(i,j)=1\) introduces multiplicative number-theoretic structure, forcing use of Möbius inversion and the Riemann zeta function.  
• Interaction of concepts: analysis (Riemann sums, dominated convergence) must be blended with analytic number theory (Dirichlet series, density of coprime pairs).  
• Deeper theory: evaluating the limit hinges on understanding \(\sum\mu(d)/d^{2}=1/\zeta(2)\), a non-elementary constant.  
• More steps: the solution demands reformulation, inversion, asymptotic analysis of weighted sums, uniform error control, and identification of an Euler product—substantially beyond the single-integral estimate in the original problem.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
f(x)=\frac{1}{1+x^{2}},\qquad x\ge 0 .
\]

For every positive integer \(n\) put  
\[
S_{n}\;=\;\frac{1}{n^{2}}
\sum_{\substack{1\le i,j\le n^{2}\\\gcd(i,j)=1}}
f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{i}{n}\Bigr)\,
f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{j}{n}\Bigr).
\]

Evaluate the limit  
\[
L \;=\;\lim_{n\to\infty} S_{n}.
\]

(The Mobius function is denoted by \(\mu\), the Riemann zeta-function by \(\zeta\).)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout \(C,C_{1},\dots\) denote positive absolute constants that may vary from line to line, and  
\[
\int f:=\int_{0}^{\infty}\!f(x)\,dx=\frac{\pi}{2}.
\]

Step 1. Mobius inversion.  
Using the identity \(\mathbf{1}_{(\gcd(i,j)=1)}=\sum_{d\mid i,\,d\mid j}\mu(d)\), we obtain  
\[
S_{n}
=\frac{1}{n^{2}}
\sum_{d=1}^{n^{2}}\mu(d)
\sum_{k=1}^{\lfloor n^{2}/d\rfloor}
\sum_{\ell=1}^{\lfloor n^{2}/d\rfloor}
f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{dk}{n}\Bigr)\,
f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{d\ell}{n}\Bigr). \tag{1}
\]

Put \(m_{d}(n):=\lfloor n^{2}/d\rfloor\).  
The inner double sum factors:
\[
\sum_{k,\ell\le m_{d}(n)}f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{dk}{n}\Bigr)
f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{d\ell}{n}\Bigr)
=\Bigl[\;\sum_{k=1}^{m_{d}(n)}f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{dk}{n}\Bigr)\Bigr]^{2}. \tag{2}
\]

Step 2. A uniform Riemann-sum estimate.  
Define  
\[
A_{d,n}:=\frac{d}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{m_{d}(n)}f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{dk}{n}\Bigr).
\]

Because \(f\) is positive, decreasing and continuous, for every \(k\ge 1\) we have  
\[
\int_{(k-1)d/n}^{kd/n}\!f(x)\,dx
\;\ge\;\frac{d}{n}f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{kd}{n}\Bigr)
\;\ge\;
\int_{kd/n}^{(k+1)d/n}\!f(x)\,dx .
\]

Summing \(k=1,\dots,m_{d}(n)\) yields  
\[
\int_{d/n}^{(m_{d}(n)+1)d/n}\!f
\;\le\;A_{d,n}
\;\le\;
\int_{0}^{m_{d}(n)d/n}\!f. \tag{3}
\]

Hence  
\[
\Bigl|A_{d,n}-\!\!\int_{0}^{m_{d}(n)d/n}\!f\Bigr|
\le 2\frac{d}{n}. \tag{4}
\]

We now show that
\[
m_{d}(n)\,\frac{d}{n}\;\ge\;\frac{n}{2}\qquad(n\ge 2,\;1\le d\le n^{2}). \tag{5}
\]

Indeed, since \(m_{d}(n)=\lfloor n^{2}/d\rfloor\ge n^{2}/d-1\),  
\[
m_{d}(n)\frac{d}{n}\;\ge\;\Bigl(\frac{n^{2}}{d}-1\Bigr)\frac{d}{n}
=\;n-\frac{d}{n}.
\]
If \(d\le n^{2}/2\) the right-hand side is at least \(n/2\).  
If \(d> n^{2}/2\) then \(m_{d}(n)=1\) and \(m_{d}(n)\tfrac{d}{n}=d/n\ge n^{2}/(2n)=n/2\).  
Thus (5) holds in all cases.

Because \(f(x)\le x^{-2}\) for \(x\ge 1\) and \(n/2\ge 1\) when \(n\ge 2\), (5) gives  
\[
\int_{m_{d}(n)d/n}^{\infty}f(x)\,dx
\;\le\;\int_{n/2}^{\infty}x^{-2}\,dx
=\frac{2}{n}. \tag{6}
\]

Combining (4) and (6) we obtain  
\[
\bigl|A_{d,n}-\tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr|
\le 2\frac{d}{n}+\frac{2}{n}\le 4\frac{d}{n}. \tag{7}
\]

A second fact needed later is the trivial bound  
\[
0\le A_{d,n}\le\int_{0}^{\infty}f=\frac{\pi}{2}. \tag{8}
\]

Step 3. Separating the main term.  
Rewrite the inner sum as  
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{m_{d}(n)}f\!\Bigl(\tfrac{dk}{n}\Bigr)
=\frac{n}{d}\Bigl(\frac{\pi}{2}+\varepsilon_{d,n}\Bigr),\qquad
|\varepsilon_{d,n}|\le 4\frac{d}{n}, \tag{9}
\]
by (7).  
Insert (9) into (1)-(2):
\[
S_{n}
=\sum_{d=1}^{n^{2}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}
\Bigl(\frac{\pi}{2}+\varepsilon_{d,n}\Bigr)^{2}. \tag{10}
\]

Step 4. Passage to the limit.

(i) Main term.  
\[
\sum_{d\le n^{2}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}
\;\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}\;
\sum_{d=1}^{\infty}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}
=\frac{1}{\zeta(2)}
=\frac{6}{\pi^{2}}. \tag{11}
\]

(ii) Linear error term.  
Using \(|\varepsilon_{d,n}|\le 4d/n\),
\[
\Bigl|\sum_{d\le n^{2}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}\varepsilon_{d,n}\Bigr|
\le\frac{4}{n}\sum_{d\le n^{2}}\frac{1}{d}
=O\!\Bigl(\tfrac{\log n}{n}\Bigr)\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}0. \tag{12}
\]

(iii) Quadratic error term.  
Define  
\[
Q_{n}:=\sum_{d\le n^{2}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}\varepsilon_{d,n}^{2}. \tag{13}
\]

Absolute convergence of \(\sum_{d\ge 1}|\,\mu(d)|/d^{2}\) is crucial.  
From (8) we have \(|\varepsilon_{d,n}|\le M:=\pi/2\).  
Fix \(\eta>0\); choose \(D=D(\eta)\) such that  
\[
\sum_{d>D}\frac{|\,\mu(d)|}{d^{2}}<\frac{\eta}{M^{2}}. \tag{14}
\]

Split (13) at \(D\):
\[
Q_{n}=Q_{n}^{(1)}+Q_{n}^{(2)},\quad
Q_{n}^{(1)}:=\sum_{d\le D}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}\varepsilon_{d,n}^{2},\;
Q_{n}^{(2)}:=\sum_{D<d\le n^{2}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2}}\varepsilon_{d,n}^{2}.
\]

For each fixed \(d\) we have \(\varepsilon_{d,n}\to 0\) by (9); hence \(Q_{n}^{(1)}\to 0\).  
For the tail use (14):
\[
|Q_{n}^{(2)}|\le M^{2}\sum_{d>D}\frac{|\,\mu(d)|}{d^{2}}<\eta. \tag{15}
\]

Because \(\eta\) is arbitrary, (15) forces \(Q_{n}^{(2)}\to 0\), thus \(Q_{n}\to 0\).

Step 5. Conclusion.  
Combining (10)-(12) and the above limit for \(Q_{n}\) we get  
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}S_{n}
=\Bigl(\frac{\pi}{2}\Bigr)^{2}\cdot\frac{1}{\zeta(2)}
=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}\cdot\frac{6}{\pi^{2}}
=\frac{3}{2}.
\]
\[
\boxed{L=\dfrac{3}{2}}
\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\;\;\;\;\square
\]
